text.heading: The ‚Äòconfessions‚Äô of Bafyau
text.p: Paschal Bafyau is a known name in Nigeria. But I am not aware if there is any other worthwhile credit to his name other than that he was a one-time president of the Nigeria Labour Congress. He later made incursions into politics and failed woefully.

After this disastrous adventure in the late 1980s and early 1990s, Bafyau had recoiled into his shell, perhaps to lick his wounds. His last political showing was his much- touted ambition to become the Vice President of Nigeria on the banner of the Social Democratic Party (SDP) in the 1993 presidential election. That election featured Moshood Kashimawo Olawale Abiola as the standard-bearer of the SDP.

But by a stroke of ill-luck or other reason, the candidacy of Bafyau was jettisoned at the last minute to pave the way for the emergence of a better positioned Babagana Kingibe. Before his emergence, Kingibe had given a good account of himself as a top civil servant, a highly valued technocrat and an accomplished diplomat. His ability to speak several foreign languages is attributed to the dexterity and intellectual gift with which God has endowed him.

In fact, many people who were hitherto not too comfortable with an Abiola-Bafyau ticket in the first instance must have heaved a sigh of relief. The argument was that Bafyau had hobnobbed too dangerously with the military oligarchy that was in power at that time and it might not be wise to entrust him with such a sensitive position. He was also reputed to be very friendly with green bottles. So, for someone who is said to be perpetually foggy upstairs, it would have been unhealthy to catapult him to the pinnacle of power.

Now, 16 years on, Bafyau, who might have secretly been bearing some grudge against Abiola since he was unceremoniously shoved aside, has at last opened up. Last week, Bafyau chose an auspicious occasion to empty his venom before an unwilling audience. The setting was a lecture organised to mark the 16th anniversary of the June 12 election organised by the Ekiti State chapter of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Ado-Ekiti last Friday.

Bafyau, who was the guest lecturer at the occasion, said that the decision by Abiola, the winner of the June 12, 1993 presidential election, to drop him as his running mate was what cost Abiola, the annulment of that election. He said his (Abiola‚Äôs) preference for Kingibe was a betrayal of trust and that the development was the genesis of the annulment of the election. Bafyau also claimed that Abiola had breached a purported agreement reached with the then military president, General Ibrahim Babangida (retd), by ditching him as his (Abiola‚Äôs) running mate.

According to Bafyau, ‚ÄúAbiola promised to announce me as his running mate on May 1, but he announced Kingibe instead. That marked the beginning of the problem of June 12 election.‚Ä? Furthermore, Bafyau stated that ‚ÄúMKO had a secret deal with the military and they gave him the condition to make me his running mate, but he reneged on the agreement. At that point, the military no longer trusted him.‚Ä? As if to further drive home his point, he said: ‚ÄúThe military saw him as somebody who could not be trusted. Babangida was not happy with him and he no longer wanted to see him. They said he was not a man of his words.‚Ä?

When Bafyau was done with his disclosure, he rubbed in some soothing balm for Abiola‚Äôs followers who would have been hurt to the marrow by his grandiloquent utterances. He described ‚ÄúAbiola as the martyr of democracy who laid his life for the democracy the country is enjoying today‚Ä?.

Beyond political rascality and cantankerous posture, I personally do not see anything that qualifies Bafyau to deliver such a lecture on such an important occasion. But I see this development as some of the truths that are yet to be unearthed on why that election which was globally adjudged to be the freest and fairest in Nigeria was annulled.

In the first instance, if not for the kind of ‚Äòpolitics of the stomach‚Äô that we play in this part of the world, how would a Bafyau have been considered the number two man in Nigeria? I do not want to sound too personal, but the truth is that I would not have been proud to point to a person as drab as Bafyau as my Vice President. Not at all.

That is why in the National Think-Tank, of which I am a founding member, we have always hammered on the type of characters we parade as leaders in this country. Most of the people who emerge as leaders in Nigeria do so because of the enormous wealth at their disposal or on the crest of the powerful, dubious and faceless people behind them. The unfortunate thing is that, today, we have drug pushers, advance fee fraudsters and all manners of individuals with despicable attitudes and behaviour parading themselves as elected or appointed officials in the corridors of power. They are everywhere.

Every day you open newspapers, you are greeted by a litany of fraudulent activities perpetrated by top government officials, I mean officials in high places who should have showed good examples by their conduct. Even at that, the system is so rotten and maggoty that most, if not all, these miscreants masquerading as government officials usually escape the wrath of the law even when they are reined in. And the looting and brigandage continue on a daily basis with such reckless abandon.

What Bafyau has simply told Nigerians is that he was one of the people who worked tirelessly to truncate Abiola‚Äôs victory in 1993. Except that he wants us to believe that when he was shoved out of the race, he simply went back home to his wife to drink fura. But that would have been an impossible option for someone who could nurse such a grudge for 16 years. He must have adopted a stance akin to a Yoruba proverb which says ‚Äúkaka ki eku ma je sese, a fi se awa danu‚Ä?, meaning ‚Äúinstead of the mouse not to eat something, it would simply mess it up to prevent others from having it‚Ä?.

Abiola might have had his own problems with the rapacious and ruthless military while he was alive, but what we all are saying is that this man won an election ‚Äúclean and clear‚Ä?. Therefore, there was no justification for its annulment. It is a pity that all we have been inundated with since the criminal annulment are a potpourri of lies, half-truths and blatant deceit from the major actors in the tragi-comedy that almost shook the nation to its foundations.
However, the most appalling thing is that we have not utilized the lessons of June 12 to shape our national life. Herein lies the real tragedy of the whole saga.
